participants.reasoning: The extracted participants match the official description of women in primary care clinics in Mexico City and participating nurses, with the same age range, IPV-related eligibility, clinic setting, and nurse recruitment criteria.
participants.extracted: The study enrolled women aged 18-44 years attending 42 public first-level health clinics in Mexico City, Mexico. Eligible participants were adult women in a current heterosexual relationship with a male partner who screened positive for past-year sexual or physical intimate partner violence, and who were not pregnant or were in the first trimester of pregnancy. Women with cognitive impairment, those seeking treatment for a life-threatening emergency, or those intending to relocate within two years were excluded. In addition, nurses at the participating clinics were involved in the intervention: morning-shift, non-field nurses from the 42 clinics were invited to receive training, with 197 nurses trained overall and 147 actively participating.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted intervention matches the official description in its core components: nurse-delivered integrated IPV screening, supportive counseling, safety planning/harm reduction, assisted referrals, booster counseling, and the corresponding training and control-condition referral-card usual care.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was a comprehensive nurse-delivered domestic violence program implemented in Mexico City public health clinics. In treatment clinics, nurses received a 3-day training plus up to three in-clinic coaching visits and role-play practice to deliver integrated IPV and health screening, supportive and nonjudgmental counseling, safety planning and harm-reduction counseling, and facilitated referral to community-based domestic violence and sexual assault resources using a prepared referral directory and referral cards. The program also included booster counseling sessions at 3 months after baseline, in which screening, referral, safety planning, and harm-reduction components were reviewed and repeated. Control clinics received usual care consisting of a brief one-day sensitization/training session for nurses and a business-card-sized referral card with general IPV information and a list of resources.
interventions.official: Intervention (n = 480):nurse‐delivered intervention to women, which involved: (1) integrated IPV and health‐screening assessment; (2) supportive care; (3) safety planning and harm reduction counselling, including reproductive health concerns; (4) assisted referrals; and (5) a booster counselling session at 3 months after screening and counselling session; intervention‐clinic nurses received 3 days of intensive training on screening for IPV and reproductive coercion (plus 3 follow‐up clinic visit

outcomes.reasoning: The extraction adds outcomes not listed officially (e.g., reproductive coercion, nurse knowledge/attitudes/skills, fidelity/process measures, readiness/self-efficacy) and also differs on timing and definitions of the primary IPV measures.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure the following outcomes: primary outcome: past-year intimate partner violence (IPV), including severe IPV (sexual or physical) and injuries from such IPV, assessed at baseline, 3-month follow-up, and 15-month follow-up using study survey instruments; secondary outcomes: past-year reproductive coercion, use of community-based resources, safety planning measures, and quality of life/mental health, assessed at baseline, 3-month follow-up, and 15-month follow-up using study survey instruments; nurse process-evaluation outcomes: knowledge, attitudes, and skills related to screening and referral for IPV, assessed pre- and post-training and then at 3-month intervals using short surveys/tests; fidelity/process outcomes: adherence to screening, referral, and counseling protocols measured during mock-client visits and exit interviews throughout the study period; additional nurse-related outcomes included readiness/self-efficacy to identify IPV and assist women with risk mitigation, assessed during training and follow-up surveys.
outcomes.official:
Primary outcomes:women's physical and sexual IPV (time point 1: IPV experienced in past 12 months; time point 2: IPV in past 3 months; and time point 3: IPV in past 15 months), measured using affirmative response to binary measure from the WHO Multicounty Study on DV and Women’s Health
Secondary outcomes
Referrals (actual), made by providers, reported by women, or taken up and reported by referred agencies (e.g. community resources used)
